symbols: bishop's vestments with crosses — the episcopal authority and the centrality of the cross to the Church
gospel book held in hand — Chrysostom's renowned preaching and exposition of Scripture
halo — sanctity and participation in divine glory
cross-patterned omophorion — the liturgical garment symbolizing the bishop as shepherd carrying the lost sheep
frontal hieratic pose — the timeless, eternal presence of the saint in Orthodox devotion
description: A tall, narrow Orthodox icon depicting a standing male figure in elaborate liturgical vestments. The figure wears a white and black cross-patterned omophorion (bishop's stole) over blue and patterned robes. He holds a gospel book in one hand and extends the other in a gesture of blessing or teaching. His face is dark-complexioned with a short dark beard and a halo. The background is gold leaf. The vestments are rendered in great detail with geometric cross and chain-link patterns. The figure fills the entire height of the narrow panel.
theological_description: Saint John Chrysostom — 'Golden Mouth' — was the Archbishop of Constantinople (398-404 AD) and is regarded as one of the greatest preachers and commentators in the history of the Church. This icon presents him in the full liturgical vestments of a bishop, affirming his authority as shepherd and teacher. The gospel book he holds represents his extraordinary devotion to Scripture — his homilies on Matthew, John, Romans, and other books remain foundational texts of biblical interpretation. Chrysostom's preaching was characterized by both accessibility and fearless moral challenge; he denounced the luxuries of the imperial court and advocated for the poor, eventually being exiled for his boldness. The cross-patterned omophorion he wears symbolizes the bishop's role as shepherd who carries the lost sheep on his shoulders, following the model of Christ. In Orthodox tradition, Chrysostom is commemorated as one of the Three Holy Hierarchs alongside Basil the Great and Gregory the Theologian. His Divine Liturgy remains the most commonly celebrated liturgical rite in the Orthodox Church.

scripture_refs.reason: Preach the word; be prepared in season and out — Chrysostom as the exemplar of faithful, bold preaching
You are the salt of the earth and light of the world — Chrysostom's moral challenge to the Church and empire
Shepherd the flock of God — the episcopal ministry Chrysostom exercised with prophetic courage
